title: Social Engineering Awareness
description: Test your ability to recognize manipulation tactics used to gain access to information, systems, or physical spaces.

choices.text: Installing software updates
Manipulating people to reveal confidential information or take unsafe actions
Encrypting files for backup
Repairing damaged hardware

questions.explanation: Social engineering relies on manipulating people rather than directly attacking technology.

questions.prompt: Why do attackers often target people instead of systems?

choices.text: People are often easier to manipulate than technical defenses
Computers cannot store data
Networks are never vulnerable
Software updates stop all attacks

questions.explanation: Attackers often exploit trust, urgency, or confusion because manipulating people can be easier than bypassing security controls.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is an example of using false authority?

choices.text: A caller claims to be from IT and asks for your password
A coworker reminds you about lunch
A calendar event shows your meeting time
A printer runs out of paper

questions.explanation: Attackers often pretend to be IT, HR, management, or vendors to pressure people into complying.

questions.prompt: What should you do if someone claims to be IT and asks for your password?

choices.text: Give it to them if they sound professional
Verify the request through official channels first
Write it down and leave it for them
Text it to them later

questions.explanation: Passwords and other sensitive information should never be shared without proper verification.

choices.text: Sharing passwords with a teammate
Following someone into a restricted area without authorization
Sending a group email too quickly
Backing up a device at the end of the day

questions.explanation: Tailgating is a physical security issue where an unauthorized person gains entry by following an authorized person.

questions.prompt: You see someone you do not recognize entering a secure area behind you. What is the best response?

choices.text: Ignore it
Assume they belong there
Politely ask for identification or report it according to policy
Hold the door open for them

questions.explanation: Unknown people should not be allowed into secure areas without proper verification.

questions.prompt: Which tactic tries to pressure you into acting immediately?

choices.text: Urgency
Encryption
Compression
Synchronization

questions.explanation: Urgency is commonly used to stop people from slowing down and verifying a request.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is an example of baiting?

choices.text: An unknown USB drive labeled 'Payroll' left in the office
A scheduled security training email from your organization
A verified update notice in your system portal
A badge scan at the front door

questions.explanation: Baiting uses curiosity or temptation to get someone to interact with something unsafe.

questions.prompt: What is the safest response when a request feels unusual or manipulative?

choices.text: Respond quickly so the person is not delayed
Pause and verify through official channels
Share only part of the requested information
Let someone else deal with it later

questions.explanation: Pausing and verifying is one of the most effective defenses against social engineering.

questions.prompt: Which of the following should never be shared in response to an unverified request?

choices.text: Your password or MFA code
A public website URL
The office lunch menu
A meeting room number already posted publicly

questions.explanation: Passwords, MFA codes, and other sensitive credentials should never be shared in response to unverified requests.
